agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle-class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This spans multiple bounded contexts: Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Lifecycle, and Customer Notification.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple bounded contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop ordering, and ETA estimation. (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, shift lifecycle, vehicle classes, zone assignments, and licensing constraints. (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, failed, completed), zone classification, and package attributes (size/weight/class). (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery windows, and customer communication preferences. The feature primarily lives in Route Planning but depends on events and data from all four contexts.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out. A shift is the temporal container within which route assignments are valid." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic area that groups pending deliveries and constrains which drivers can be assigned to them. A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize total travel time/distance within a zone, subject to vehicle-class and time constraints." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and requires the system to recompute stop ordering. Three defined triggers: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been accepted into the system but not yet assigned to a route or not yet attempted. The pool from which routes are built." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (e.g., recipient unavailable, address inaccessible). Removed from the active route and triggers recalculation." },
      { "term": "Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of both the driver's licensed vehicle and the delivery's transport requirement (e.g., bicycle, motorcycle, van, freight truck). Used to enforce physical and legal compatibility." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "The set of the three upcoming delivery stops on a driver's active route. Entry into this window triggers customer notification." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Driver Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the system-suggested route beyond a tolerance threshold, indicating they are no longer following the plan." },
      { "term": "Route Assignment", "definition": "The act of binding an optimized route to a driver's active shift. Only one active route per driver at a time." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver must be assigned deliveries only within their zone for the current shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is the fundamental partitioning mechanism. Cross-zone assignment would corrupt route optimization, create driver conflicts, and violate operational territory boundaries.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.zone == driver.currentShift.zone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver cannot be assigned a delivery whose required vehicle class exceeds their licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This encodes a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle cannot carry freight. Violating this results in undeliverable assignments and potential legal/safety liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass is compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in their zone that are compatible with their vehicle class.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The automatic assignment at shift start is a business policy. The system could alternatively allow manual route building or deferred assignment. This is a workflow decision, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Within N seconds of a driver starting a shift, a route is created containing all eligible pending deliveries in their zone, ordered by optimization algorithm."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation policy could be changed to batch recalculations or manual triggers. The trigger is a business decision about freshness vs. computational cost.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a new delivery enters a zone with an active driver route, the route is recalculated and the new delivery is included if vehicle-class compatible."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation on failure is a policy choice. The system could alternatively just skip the stop without reoptimizing the remaining sequence.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery on an active route is marked failed, the failed delivery is removed and remaining stops are reoptimized."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Deviation-triggered recalculation is a policy. Some systems simply let the driver return to the route. The threshold for 'deviation' is also configurable.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver deviation is detected beyond the defined threshold, the remaining stops are reoptimized from the driver's current position."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The notification window size (3) and the decision to notify at all are business policies that could be changed by product or ops.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery moves into position 1, 2, or 3 on the active route, and the customer has not already been notified for this delivery attempt, a notification is sent."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes, overridable per zone by the operations team.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Both the default value and the override mechanism are operational policies. These will change as the business learns from real data.",
      "testable_assertion": "Route ETA calculations use the zone-specific stop duration if configured, otherwise 4 minutes. Only users with operations role can override the value."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A driver can have at most one active route at a time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Multiple simultaneous active routes for one driver is a logical impossibility — a driver can only be at one location executing one sequence. Allowing multiple routes would corrupt all ETA calculations and customer notifications.",
      "testable_assertion": "At any point in time, COUNT(activeRoutes where route.driverId == driver.id) <= 1."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "A delivery that is already assigned to an active route cannot be simultaneously assigned to another driver's route.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Double-assignment of a delivery would result in two drivers attempting the same delivery, wasting resources and confusing the customer. This is a fundamental data consistency constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery with status 'assigned', exactly one active route contains that delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-11",
      "rule": "Recalculation must not reorder or remove already-completed stops from the route history.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Completed stops are historical facts. Rewriting them would corrupt delivery records, proof-of-delivery timestamps, and audit trails.",
      "testable_assertion": "After any recalculation, all previously completed stops remain in the route with their original completion data, and only future stops are subject to reordering."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 10 pending deliveries in their zone, 8 compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route is generated containing exactly the 8 compatible deliveries, ordered by the optimization algorithm, and the 2 incompatible deliveries remain in the pending pool"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 7 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new delivery is added to their zone that is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, all remaining stops are reoptimized from the driver's current position, and completed stops are preserved"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and the 4th stop is marked as failed",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The failed delivery is removed from the active route, remaining stops are reoptimized, and the failed delivery's status is updated in the Delivery context"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver deviates from the suggested route beyond the configured threshold",
      "when": "The deviation is detected",
      "then": "The remaining stops are reoptimized starting from the driver's actual current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver completes a stop and a delivery moves from position 4 to position 3 on the route",
      "when": "The route position is updated",
      "then": "The customer for the delivery now at position 3 receives a notification with an estimated arrival time"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A customer's delivery is already in the 'next 3 stops' window and a recalculation keeps it within that window",
      "when": "The recalculation completes",
      "then": "The customer is NOT sent a duplicate notification for the same delivery attempt"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A motorcycle driver is on shift in a zone",
      "when": "A freight delivery is added to that zone",
      "then": "The freight delivery is NOT added to the motorcycle driver's route, regardless of recalculation"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "The operations team has set the stop duration for Zone A to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A",
      "then": "ETA calculations use 6 minutes per stop, not the 4-minute default"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A driver has an active route",
      "when": "A recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress",
      "then": "The system handles the concurrency safely — either queues the second trigger or cancels the in-flight calculation and restarts with the latest state"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "Two drivers are active in the same zone",
      "when": "Routes are generated or recalculated",
      "then": "No delivery appears on more than one driver's active route simultaneously"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift with zero pending deliveries in their zone", "expected_behavior": "An empty route is created (or no route). When the first delivery enters the zone, a route is generated. The driver is not left in a broken state." },
    { "scenario": "All pending deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver possesses", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries remain in the pending pool unassigned. The system should surface this as an operational alert — deliveries are accumulating with no eligible driver." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added to a zone with multiple active drivers", "expected_behavior": "The system must decide which driver gets the delivery. This requires a partitioning or load-balancing rule not specified in the current requirements." },
    { "scenario": "Recalculation triggered while driver is mid-delivery at a stop", "expected_behavior": "The current stop must not be interrupted. Recalculation reorders only stops AFTER the current one. The driver's in-progress delivery is treated as fixed." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates, then returns to the original route before recalculation completes", "expected_behavior": "System should detect the return and either cancel or complete the recalculation. Debouncing or a grace period may be needed to avoid thrashing." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification delivery enters then exits the 'next 3' window due to recalculation", "expected_behavior": "Notification was already sent and cannot be recalled. The customer may receive an updated ETA or a notification that their delivery window has changed." },
    { "scenario": "A new delivery is added to a zone right as a driver is completing their last stop", "expected_behavior": "System must decide: extend the driver's shift/route, or leave the delivery for the next driver. This is a policy decision not covered in the spec." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's vehicle class changes mid-shift (e.g., vehicle swap due to breakdown)", "expected_behavior": "Route must be recalculated with the new vehicle class constraints. Incompatible deliveries already on the route must be unassigned and returned to the pending pool." },
    { "scenario": "Concurrent recalculation triggers (e.g., a delivery fails AND a new delivery enters the zone simultaneously)", "expected_behavior": "The system must coalesce or serialize these triggers to avoid race conditions that could produce inconsistent route state." },
    { "scenario": "Zone boundary edge — delivery address is on the border of two zones", "expected_behavior": "The delivery must be assigned to exactly one zone. The zone assignment logic is upstream of route planning and must be deterministic." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, driver hours, and missed delivery penalties. Dynamic recalculation captures new revenue opportunities (new deliveries absorbed into existing routes). Incorrect vehicle-class assignment could result in failed deliveries, wasted driver trips, and potential regulatory fines.",
    "operational": "Significant reduction in dispatcher workload — manual route planning is eliminated. However, the system introduces computational load from frequent recalculations. Operations teams gain a new lever (stop duration override) to tune zone performance. The recalculation frequency must be managed to avoid route instability ('thrashing') that confuses drivers.",
    "user": "Customers gain transparency via the 'next 3 stops' notification — improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my package' support calls. Drivers benefit from clear, optimized directions but may experience frustration if routes change too frequently mid-shift. Trust in the system depends on route stability.",
    "risk": "HIGH: Multi-driver zone assignment is unspecified — this is a critical gap that could lead to delivery duplication or starvation. MEDIUM: Deviation detection threshold is undefined — too sensitive causes thrashing, too loose makes the feature useless. MEDIUM: No mention of route optimization algorithm constraints (time vs. distance vs. priority) — different algorithms yield very different business outcomes.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary workflow change. Routes are now system-driven rather than self-directed or dispatcher-assigned. Frequent recalculations could reduce trust and increase cognitive load." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Positive — proactive notifications improve experience. Risk of notification noise if recalculations cause deliveries to oscillate in/out of the 3-stop window." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains zone-level stop duration control. Loses direct route control. Needs monitoring tools for unassignable deliveries and route health." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Role fundamentally changes from route planners to exception handlers. May need retraining or role redefinition." },
      { "group": "Finance/Business", "impact": "Expects cost savings from optimization. Needs KPIs to measure: deliveries per shift, fuel cost per delivery, on-time rate." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec covers the happy path well but has critical gaps: (1) multi-driver zone assignment logic is completely unspecified — this is the core algorithmic decision and cannot be inferred, (2) deviation detection threshold is undefined, (3) no specification for how route optimization balances competing objectives (speed vs. distance vs. priority), (4) no handling defined for shift-end scenarios. These gaps affect the core feature behavior.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class enforcement is a clear, hard invariant with no ambiguity",
      "Customer notification trigger at 'next 3 stops' is well-defined",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is clear",
      "The three recalculation triggers are explicitly enumerated"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver zone partitioning — how deliveries are divided among concurrent drivers in the same zone",
      "Deviation detection — what constitutes a 'deviation' and the threshold/mechanism",
      "Route optimization objective function — minimize time? distance? prioritize certain deliveries?",
      "Shift-end boundary behavior — what happens to undelivered stops when a shift ends",
      "Recalculation concurrency model — how simultaneous triggers are handled"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy (motorcycle < van < truck), not a matrix of arbitrary compatibility",
      "Zone assignment of deliveries happens upstream and is not part of route optimization",
      "One driver per zone is the common case, but multi-driver zones must be supported",
      "The optimization algorithm is a black box to this domain analysis — we define WHAT it must respect, not HOW it optimizes",
      "Customer notification is a one-time event per delivery attempt entering the window, not repeated on recalculation"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Multi-driver zone assignment is unspecified. When multiple drivers are active in the same zone, how are pending deliveries partitioned among them? Options include geographic sub-clustering, round-robin, vehicle-class-first matching, or workload balancing. This is the single most critical design decision for the feature and it is entirely absent from the spec.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "Geographic sub-clustering: divide zone into micro-regions per driver",
        "Workload balancing: assign to driver with fewest remaining stops",
        "Vehicle-class-first: assign to the cheapest-capable vehicle class first",
        "Manual dispatcher override: let dispatchers partition, system optimizes within partition"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Vehicle-class-first with workload balancing as tiebreaker. This minimizes cost (don't send a truck when a motorcycle will do) while distributing work fairly. Escalate to product team for confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Deviation detection threshold is undefined. What GPS distance or time deviation constitutes 'manually deviates from the suggested route'? Too sensitive (50m) causes constant recalculation thrashing. Too loose (2km) makes the feature meaningless. This also interacts with urban vs. rural zones differently.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "Fixed distance threshold (e.g., 500m from expected route)",
        "Time-based threshold (e.g., 5 minutes behind expected ETA at next stop)",
        "Sequence-based: driver visits a stop out of suggested order",
        "Configurable per zone by operations team, like stop duration"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Sequence-based deviation (driver skips a stop or visits out of order) as the primary trigger, with a configurable distance threshold as secondary. Sequence deviation is unambiguous and directly impacts route optimality."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Route optimization objective function is not defined. Minimize total distance? Minimize total time? Prioritize time-sensitive deliveries? Respect delivery time windows promised to customers? The optimization algorithm's objective fundamentally changes which route is 'correct.'",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Drivers", "Finance/Business"],
      "options": [
        "Minimize total travel time (standard TSP)",
        "Minimize total distance (fuel cost optimization)",
        "Prioritize deliveries with customer-promised time windows",
        "Weighted multi-objective combining all above"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Weighted multi-objective with customer time windows as hard constraints and travel time as the optimization target. This balances cost efficiency with customer commitments. Requires product team to define priority weights."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Shift-end behavior is undefined. When a driver's shift ends but deliveries remain on their route, what happens? Are remaining deliveries returned to the pending pool? Can a driver extend their shift? Are deliveries handed off to the next driver? This directly affects customer notification accuracy.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "Remaining deliveries return to pending pool automatically",
        "Driver is prompted to extend or hand off",
        "Operations team manually reassigns"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Remaining deliveries automatically return to the pending pool and become eligible for the next driver's route generation. Customers in the 'next 3' window who were notified should receive an updated notification. Escalate to product for confirmation."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
